stem: The symmetric matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[b]] & [[a]] \end{pmatrix} \) is diagonalized as \( A = PDP^{-1} \), with the eigenvalues of \( D \) listed in DECREASING order down the diagonal. What is \( D_{11} \)?
solution: The eigenvalues of \( A \) are \( a+b \) and \( a-b \); here these are \( [[a]]+[[b]] \) and \( [[a]]-[[b]] \). Since \( [[b]]>0 \), the larger one goes in \( D_{11} \): \( D_{11} = [[a]]+[[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the smaller eigenvalue \( a-b \), which belongs in \( D_{22} \), not \( D_{11} \).
This is the shared diagonal entry of \( A \) itself, the average of the two eigenvalues, not an eigenvalue.
This doubles the diagonal entry rather than adding the correct offset \( b \).
This is the off-diagonal entry of \( A \), not an eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct value of \( D_{11} \).
This is two more than the correct value of \( D_{11} \).

stem: \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a11]] & [[a12]] \\ [[a21]] & [[a22]] \end{pmatrix} \) is diagonalizable as \( A = PDP^{-1} \). Since \( \operatorname{tr}(D) = \operatorname{tr}(A) \), what is the sum of the diagonal entries of \( D \)?
solution: Similar matrices share the same trace, so \( \operatorname{tr}(D) = \operatorname{tr}(A) = [[a11]]+[[a22]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \det(A) \), which equals \( \det(D) \), not \( \operatorname{tr}(D) \).
This multiplies the diagonal entries instead of adding them.
This sums the off-diagonal entries instead of the diagonal entries.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a11]] & [[a12]] \\ [[a21]] & [[a22]] \end{pmatrix} \) is diagonalizable as \( A = PDP^{-1} \). Since \( \det(D) = \det(A) \), what is the product of the diagonal entries of \( D \)?
solution: Similar matrices share the same determinant, so the product of \( D \)'s diagonal entries is \( \det(D) = \det(A) = ([[a11]])([[a22]]) - ([[a12]])([[a21]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \operatorname{tr}(A) \), which equals \( \operatorname{tr}(D) \), not \( \det(D) \).
This omits the off-diagonal product \( a_{12}a_{21} \) entirely.
This is the negative of the determinant (the two products were swapped).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The triangular matrix \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ 0 & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[f]] \end{pmatrix} \) is diagonalizable as \( A = PDP^{-1} \), with the eigenvalues of \( D \) listed in INCREASING order down the diagonal. What is \( D_{11} \)?
solution: The eigenvalues of the triangular matrix \( A \) are exactly its diagonal entries \( [[a]], [[d]], [[f]] \); the smallest of these goes into \( D_{11} \) since \( D \) is arranged in increasing order: \( D_{11} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one of the three eigenvalues, but not necessarily the smallest.
This is another of the three eigenvalues, but not necessarily the smallest.
This is another of the three eigenvalues, but not necessarily the smallest.
This is the trace (sum of all eigenvalues), not the smallest one.
This is one more than the correct smallest eigenvalue.
This is one less than the correct smallest eigenvalue.

stem: A diagonalizable matrix \( A = PDP^{-1} \) has \( D = \begin{pmatrix} [[d1]] & 0 \\ 0 & [[d2]] \end{pmatrix} \). Using \( A^{[[k]]} = PD^{[[k]]}P^{-1} \), what is \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{[[k]]}) \)?
solution: Since \( A^{[[k]]} \) is similar to \( D^{[[k]]} = \operatorname{diag}([[d1]]^{[[k]]}, [[d2]]^{[[k]]}) \), they share the same trace: \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{[[k]]}) = [[d1]]^{[[k]]} + [[d2]]^{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This raises the trace of \( A \) to the \( [[k]] \)-th power first, which is not the same as \( \operatorname{tr}(A^{[[k]]}) \) in general.
This is \( \det(A^{[[k]]}) \) (the product of the powered eigenvalues), not the trace.
This averages the two powered eigenvalues instead of adding them.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
